title: Excess mortality: Raw number of deaths from all causes compared to projection based on previous years
subtitle: The reported number of weekly or monthly deaths in 2020–2024 and the projected number of deaths for 2020, which is based on the reported deaths in 2015–2019.
caption:
Excess mortality: Raw number of deaths from all causes compared to projection based on previous years

The reported number of weekly or monthly deaths in 2020–2024 and the projected number of deaths for 2020, which is based on the reported deaths in 2015–2019.

The chart displays weekly death counts in the United States from January 2020 through early 2021, comparing actual reported figures against a projected baseline. The projection for 2020, derived from 2015–2019 data, appears as a black line hovering consistently between 50,000 and 60,000 deaths per week. Historical data from 2015 through 2019 form a cluster of gray lines that generally track below or alongside this projection, mostly staying within the 50,000 to 65,000 range. In contrast, the 2020 data, shown in orange, exhibits two distinct surges above the projection. The first peak occurs around April 2020, reaching approximately 80,000 deaths, while a second, larger rise begins in late 2020, exceeding 80,000 and approaching 90,000 by January 2021. The year 2021, represented by a purple line, starts the period with values between 80,000 and 90,000 before declining to join the 60,000 to 70,000 band by mid-year. Data for 2022 and 2023, shown in teal and blue respectively, largely remain within the 55,000 to 65,000 range, tracking closer to the historical baseline. The reported number of deaths might not count all deaths that occurred due to incomplete coverage and delays in reporting.
